images.caption: Figure 1:MobileMoE establishes a new Pareto frontier for on-device LLMs.Average benchmark accuracy, computed over 14 benchmarks spanning commonsense, knowledge, science, comprehension, and reasoning, is plotted against (a) per-token inference computeFinf=2​NactF_{\text{inf}}=2N_{\text{act}}(GFLOPs) and (b) total parametersNtotalN_{\text{total}}(B); in (b), x-axis tick labels show total params (B)||projected INT4 memory (GB)222Accuracy is reported from public model releases at full precision (BF16). In subplot (b), the x-axis shows total parametersNtotalN_{\text{total}}in billions alongside the corresponding projected INT4 weight memory, computed asℳweight=12​Ntotal\mathcal{M}_{\text{weight}}=\tfrac{1}{2}N_{\text{total}}GB under 4-bit weight quantization that is commonly used for on-device LLM deployment (as detailed in Section3.4, Section4.3)..

Figure 2:MobileMoE architectures.Left: MoE model design space with three design factors: model sparsity(E,k)(E,k), expert granularitygg, shared expertsss.Right: MobileMoE at three scales: Small (S), Medium (M), Large (L) with 0.3, 0.5, 0.9B active parameters, using a base architecture of expansion ratiodff/dmodel=4d_{\text{ff}}/d_{\text{model}}=4, aspect ratiodmodel/nl≈40d_{\text{model}}/n_{l}\approx 40, SwiGLU, GQA with 4 KV heads, along with variants of MoE design choices on number of expertsE∈{1,2,4,8,16,32}E\in\{1,2,4,8,16,32\}, fine-grained expert granularityg∈{1,2,4,8,16}g\in\{1,2,4,8,16\}, and shared expert {✓, ×}.

Figure 3:Scaling the number of expertsEE.Model loss predicted by the fitted on-device scaling law (Eq. (1)) plotted against (a) total parametersNtotalN_{\text{total}}, (b) on-device memoryℳ\mathcal{M}(Eq. (5)), and (c) active parametersNactN_{\text{act}}, under a training budget of5×10205\times 10^{20}FLOPs (our experimental regime). Each curve corresponds to one expert countEE, truncated at the crossover with the next-largerEEcurve. Shaded regions indicate the optimalEEalong each axis; e.g., atℳ=5\mathcal{M}=5GB, the optimalEEis 8, marked by the star in (b).

Figure 4:Scaling MoE models with compute optimality.The compute-optimal loss vs. training compute FLOPs for each MoE design factor predicted by the on-device scaling law (Eq. (1)): when varying (a) number of expertsEE, (b) expert granularitygg, (c) with vs without shared expert, the curves with lower model loss indicate the more compute-efficient design choices given a fixed compute budget. Grey area is the experimental regime.

Figure 5:Training efficiency of MoE architecture(Nact=0.3N_{\text{act}}=0.3B). Training loss vs. wall-clock hours when varying (a) number of expertsE∈{1,2,4,8,16,32}E\in\{1,2,4,8,16,32\}, (b) expert granularityg∈{1,2,4,8,16}g\in\{1,2,4,8,16\}atE=8E=8, and (c) with vs. without a shared experts∈{✓,×}s\in\{\checkmark,\times\}atE=8,g=8E=8,g=8. For fair comparison, each run is trained on the same data for 500B tokens, with identical hardware (8 nodes, 64 NVIDIA H100 96 GB GPUs) – see Table6.1for full ablation details.

Figure 6:MobileMoE four-stage training recipe:pre-training (PT)→\rightarrowmid-training (MT)→\rightarrowinstruct supervised fine-tuning (SFT)→\rightarrowquantization-aware training (QAT) with INT4 precision.

Figure 7:MobileMoE training data mixtures across stages.Domain composition of pre-training (PT,∼\sim6T tokens), mid-training (MT,∼\sim500B tokens), and supervised fine-tuning (SFT,>>80M samples), with consistent colors per domain across stages. Across stages, the mixture transitions from web-heavy coverage in PT toward domain-specific data (knowledge, code, math) in MT and SFT. Full per-domain datasets are listed in Tables7.1,7.2, and7.3.

Figure 8:MobileMoE pre-training trajectories.Average benchmark accuracy of MobileMoE-S/M/L across five core competencies plus overall average, plotted against training tokensDDup to 6T. Horizontal lines: publicly released pre-trained models with reported pre-training token budgets (i.e., Llama 3.2 1B: 9T, with distillation from Llama 3.1 8B; OLMoE-1B-7B: 5T; OLMo 2-1B: 4T; SmolLM2-1.7B: 11T). Evaluation setups are given in Table8.1.

Figure 9:MobileMoE-Base model comparison.Average benchmark accuracy of MobileMoE-S/M/L after mid-training across five core competencies plus overall average, vs. publicly released base models (Gemma 3, SmolLM2, Qwen3.5, MobileLLM-Pro, Llama 3.2, OLMoE, OLMo 2), plotted against active parametersNactN_{\text{act}}. Green stars: MobileMoE-S/M/L; dashed line: MobileMoE Pareto curve. Full table results are given in Table2.

Figure 10:MobileMoE capability progression across training stages.Benchmark accuracy (%) for MobileMoE-S/M/L after each of the three main training stages: pre-training (PT, light), mid-training (MT, medium), and instruction supervised fine-tuning (SFT, dark). Gray dashed line: instruct-tuned OLMoE-1B-7B (1.3B active) as a scale-matched MoE reference baseline. Each per-competence panel (Commonsense, Knowledge, Science, Reading, Reasoning) reports the average over its constituent benchmarks; the overall panel reports the average over all 14 benchmarks. Full per-benchmark numerical results are in Table5.

Figure 9.1:MobileMoE expert utilization heatmaps across training stages and domains.Each heatmap shows per-layer (rows) per-expert (columns) activation utilization on the log10scale (blue: dormant, red: hot) for MobileMoE-S evaluated on three downstream domains. Two patterns emerge: (i) different domains activate distinct expert subsets (cross-task specialization), and (ii) expert utilization broadens progressively from PT through MT to SFT.

Figure 9.2:Distribution of expert utilization across tasks: math, code, knowledge, combined (all tasks).Statistics of expert utilization ratios (log10scale) are shown across all MoE layers for MobileMoE-S (post SFT).
